    at async Bot.loop (G:\AI\telegram-kiro-bridge-main\node_modules\grammy\out\bot.js:395:33)
    at async Bot.start (G:\AI\telegram-kiro-bridge-main\node_modules\grammy\out\bot.js:316:9)
    at async main (G:\AI\telegram-kiro-bridge-main\src\index.ts:1385:3) {
  method: 'getUpdates',
  payload: {
    offset: 1,
    limit: undefined,
    timeout: 30,
    allowed_updates: [ 'message', 'callback_query', 'message_reaction' ]
  },

}

[bridge exited with code 1, restarting in 3s - Ctrl+C to stop]</user_request>

</observed_from_primary_session>

You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

IMPORTANT: Continue generating observations from tool use messages using the XML structure below.

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING CONTINUED
===========================

PROGRESS SUMMARY CHECKPOINT
===========================
Write progress notes of what was done, what was learned, and what's next. This is a checkpoint to capture progress so far. The session is ongoing - you may receive more requests and tool executions after this summary. Write "next_steps" as the current trajectory of work (what's actively being worked on or coming up next), not as post-session future work. Always write at least a minimal summary explaining current progress, even if work is still in early stages, so that users see a summary output tied to each request.

Claude's Full Response to User:
修正完成，驗證結果如下。

## 已完成

1. **修正 `src/acpClient.ts:buildSpawnEnv()`**：spawn 子 agent 時把 `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` 覆寫成空字串。效果雙重——plugin 不會繼承 bridge 的 token（不再 409 打死宿主），也不會 fallback 用它自己設定檔的 bot（避免跟你 Cursor 互動 session 的 telegram channel 互踢）。headless agent session 會直接停用 telegram plugin，這本來就是正確行為。
2. **型別檢查通過**（`npx tsc --noEmit` exit 0）。
3. **清理殘留行程**：11:18 的孤兒 agent 鏈、跑舊碼的 bridge 實例都已終止；Cursor 的 plugin（poll 另一個 bot，合法用途）保留不動。
4. **驗證**：start.bat 自動用新碼重生了 bridge（PID 36772，11:31:25 啟動），**已穩定存活 97 秒以上**——修正前的行為是啟動後十幾秒內就被 409 打死。目前 Telegram 連線只剩 bridge 自己和 Cursor plugin 各連各的 bot，token 不再被搶。

## 剩最後一步請你做

從 Telegram 傳一則訊息給 bot 測試完整流程——bridge 收到訊息會 spawn agent session，這是舊碼發病的確切時點；新碼下 plugin 會自我停用。如果 bot 正常回覆、console 沒再出現 409，就是徹底解決。

確認 OK 後說一聲，我再把這個修正 commit（目前改動只在 working tree，沒動你原本就修改中的 `docs/usage-guide.html`）。

一個遺留觀察（不動，僅回報）：`src/provider/mcp-pool.ts:56` 也把完整 `process.env` 傳給 MCP 子行程——MCP server 不會啟動 Claude plugin，所以不是這次的衝突向量，但 token 外洩面仍在，之後若要收斂可以一併處理。

Respond in this XML format:
<summary>
  <request>[Short title capturing the user's request AND the substance of what was discussed/done]</request>
  <investigated>[What has been explored so far? What was examined?]</investigated>
  <learned>[What have you learned about how things work?]</learned>
  <completed>[What work has been completed so far? What has shipped or changed?]</completed>
  <next_steps>[What are you actively working on or planning to work on next in this session?]</next_steps>
  <notes>[Additional insights or observations about the current progress]</notes>
</summary>

IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this next PROGRESS SUMMARY - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the summary content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful summary content.

Thank you, this summary will be very useful for keeping track of our progress!

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking:
The user in the primary session is experiencing an error with a Telegram bot bridge application. The error message indicates:

```
409: Conflict: terminated by other getUpdates request; make sure that only one bot instance is running
```

This is a common Telegram bot error that occurs when multiple instances of the same bot are trying to poll for updates simultaneously. Telegram's getUpdates API only allows one active long-polling connection per bot at a time.

Let me create an observation about this discovery/problem:

- **Type**: This is a "discovery" - the user is discovering/encountering a problem with their Telegram bridge application
- **Title**: Should capture the core issue - "Telegram Bot Bridge Encountering Multiple Instance Conflict"
- **Subtitle**: One sentence explaining the problem
- **Facts**: 
  - The bridge application is using Grammy framework for Telegram bot
  - Error code 409 indicates conflict from multiple getUpdates requests
  - The application is located at G:\AI\telegram-kiro-bridge-main\
  - The error occurs in src\index.ts at line 1385
  - The bridge restarts automatically after exit
- **Narrative**: Full context about what's happening and why this matters
- **Concepts**: This is about problem-solution (identifying a problem), gotcha (common trap), and how-it-works (understanding the mechanism)
- **Files**: The main file involved is src/index.ts

Let me construct this properly:

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Telegram Bot Bridge Failing Due to Multiple Instance Conflict</title>
  <subtitle>Bridge crashes with 409 error because multiple bot instances are polling Telegram simultaneously</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Bridge application uses Grammy framework for Telegram bot integration</fact>
    <fact>Error 409 "Conflict: terminated by other getUpdates request" indicates multiple instances polling simultaneously</fact>
    <fact>Telegram's getUpdates API permits only one active long-polling connection per bot token</fact>
    <fact>Application exits with code 1 and auto-restarts after 3 seconds</fact>
    <fact>Bot configuration polls for message, callback_query, and message_reaction updates</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The telegram-kiro-bridge application is crashing with a Grammy error when calling getUpdates on the Telegram Bot API. The 409 Conflict status code indicates that another bot instance is already polling for updates using the same bot token. This is a fundamental limitation of Telegram's long-polling mechanism - only one connection can actively fetch updates at a time. The error originates from src/index.ts line 1385 during the main() function execution. The auto-restart mechanism creates a loop where the bridge repeatedly fails if the conflicting instance isn't terminated. Common causes include: another process running the same bot, a previous instance that didn't shut down cleanly, webhook mode active while trying to poll, or the bot running in another environment with the same token. Resolution requires identifying and stopping all other instances before restarting the bridge.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```

1. The user reported a 409 Conflict error from Telegram Bot API - multiple instances were trying to poll for updates simultaneously
2. Claude identified the root cause: when the bridge spawns child agent sessions, they inherit the TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN environment variable, causing multiple bot instances to compete for the same token
3. Claude fixed `src/acpClient.ts:buildSpawnEnv()` to override `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` with an empty string when spawning child agents
4. Type checking passed

content.text:
```xml
<summary>
  <request>Diagnose and fix Telegram bridge 409 Conflict error caused by multiple bot instances polling simultaneously</request>
  <investigated>Bridge application crashes with Grammy error 409 "terminated by other getUpdates request" when spawning child agent sessions. Root cause traced to child agents inheriting TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN environment variable from parent bridge process, creating multiple competing polling connections to Telegram API.</investigated>
  <learned>Telegram's getUpdates API permits only one active long-polling connection per bot token. The bridge's acpClient.ts was passing complete process.env to spawned agent sessions, causing child agents to activate their Telegram plugins with the same token. This created a conflict loop where bridge restarts and agent spawns continuously fought for the polling connection.</learned>
  <completed>Fixed buildSpawnEnv() in src/acpClient.ts to override TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN with empty string when spawning child agents. This prevents plugin inheritance conflicts and avoids fallback to agent's own bot configuration. Type checking passed with npx tsc --noEmit. Cleaned up orphaned agent processes and old bridge instances. New bridge instance (PID 36772) running stably for 97+ seconds without 409 errors.</completed>
  <next_steps>Awaiting user test: send Telegram message to bot to verify full flow including agent spawn works without 409 conflicts. After confirmation, commit the fix to repository (working tree changes only, excluding unrelated docs/usage-guide.html modifications).</next_steps>
  <notes>Similar environment variable leakage pattern identified in src/provider/mcp-pool.ts:56 but not currently causing conflicts since MCP servers don't activate Claude plugin. Could be addressed in future hardening pass.</notes>
</summary>
```

content.thinking:
The user committed the fix to the repository. Let me create an observation for this commit.

Key points:
- Committed the acpClient.ts fix
- Commit SHA: e60b74764b00e6e3bed66c14cbb48ccf12c5383a
- The commit message is in Chinese and explains the root cause and fix
- Root cause: buildSpawnEnv passed complete process.env to child agent processes, causing Claude Code's telegram plugin to inherit the bridge's bot token and start competing getUpdates poller
- Fix: Override TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN with empty string in spawn env so plugin sees it as defined but empty, self-disables without fallback
- Also updated 2 skill files in default-skills/ (sync-skills hook ran)
- 3 files changed total: the two skill files and acpClient.ts

This is a bugfix type observation.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Fixed 409 Telegram Conflict by Clearing Bot Token in Child Agent Environments</title>
  <subtitle>Bridge no longer crashes when spawning child agents due to competing Telegram polling connections</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>buildSpawnEnv() in src/acpClient.ts now overrides TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN with empty string when spawning child agents</fact>
    <fact>Root cause was complete process.env inheritance allowing Claude Code telegram plugin to activate in child agents with parent's token</fact>
    <fact>Telegram API enforces single getUpdates poller per bot token, causing 409 Conflict errors when multiple instances competed</fact>
    <fact>Empty string token value causes plugin to self-disable without falling back to its own configuration file</fact>
    <fact>Fix committed as e60b74764b00e6e3bed66c14cbb48ccf12c5383a with detailed Chinese root cause analysis</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The telegram-kiro-bridge was experiencing fatal 409 Conflict errors in an infinite crash loop whenever it spawned child agent sessions. Investigation revealed that buildSpawnEnv() was passing the complete process.env to spawned claude-agent-acp child processes. Claude Code's official telegram plugin, which prioritizes environment variables over its configuration file, inherited the bridge's TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN and automatically started its own getUpdates poller. Since Telegram only permits one active polling connection per bot token, the child agent's plugin competed with the parent bridge, causing the bridge to be terminated with 409 errors and enter an endless restart cycle via start.bat. The fix modifies buildSpawnEnv() to explicitly override TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN with an empty string in the child environment. The plugin interprets this as defined-but-empty, preventing fallback to its own configuration and causing immediate self-disablement. This eliminates token conflicts both with the parent bridge and with any interactive Claude Code sessions (like Cursor) that may be using the plugin's own bot token legitimately.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/acpClient.ts</file>
    <file>default-skills/ms-agent-scheduled-prompts/SKILL.md</file>
    <file>default-skills/ms-portable-skill-authoring/SKILL.md</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```
